extra.message: User-controlled path parameter 'username' is interpolated via f-string directly into a raw SQL query executed through SQLAlchemy's text(). When vuln=1, any caller can inject arbitrary SQL (e.g., ' OR 1=1 --) via the /users/v1/{username} GET endpoint.

When vuln=True, get_by_title() retrieves a book by title without verifying that the authenticated user owns the book. Any authenticated user can access any book's secret content regardless of ownership, constituting an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR).

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: When vuln=True and the request body contains 'admin':true, the register_user() endpoint directly assigns the value to the new User's admin field. This allows any unauthenticated user to self-register with administrative privileges via mass assignment.

When vuln=True, update_password() changes the password of the user specified in the URL path parameter 'username' regardless of the authenticated user's identity. An attacker can change any user's password by providing a valid token for any account and targeting a different user in the URL.

The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as the literal string 'random'. This means JWT tokens can be forged by anyone who knows the key (which is in the public source code). All authentication tokens are trivially forgeable.

The Flask application is run with debug=True on host 0.0.0.0. In production, this exposes the interactive debugger (Werkzeug debugger) which allows remote code execution via the debug console and leaks sensitive information through stack traces.

The /users/v1/_debug endpoint via get_all_users_debug() returns all users' passwords, emails, usernames, and admin status without authentication. This exposes all user credentials to anyone who discovers the endpoint.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: User passwords are stored as plain-text String columns in the database (no hashing). The password field is a VARCHAR column and passwords are stored and compared in plaintext (see api_views/users.py line 93: request_data.get('password') == user.password). An attacker with database access can read all passwords.

cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

extra.message: When vuln=True, the login endpoint returns distinct error messages for 'Password is not correct for the given username' vs 'Username does not exist'. This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames via the response difference.

When vuln=True, the email validation regex ^([0-9a-zA-Z]([-.\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z])*@{1}([0-9a-zA-Z][-\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z]\.)+[a-zA-Z]{2,9})$ contains nested quantifiers ([-.\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z])* that can cause catastrophic backtracking (ReDoS). An attacker can send a crafted long string causing excessive CPU consumption and denial of service.
